extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities**, ensuring **continuous synchronization** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts with **sub-second latency requirements** for critical operational monitoring and control. This synchronization is facilitated through a **runtime configuration management** mechanism, enabling adaptive temporal alignment without full redeployment. The framework supports **dynamic time scales** for various services (e.g., real-time monitoring vs. predictive analytics), with no explicit granularity specifications provided in the documents, but implying high-frequency updates (e.g., milliseconds for control loops) for operational DTs. The system bridges **BIM’s static representations** with **dynamic operational needs**, requiring seamless temporal coupling between discrete BIM elements and continuous operational data streams.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system operates with a **modular, centralized architecture** where a single **DT Framework** serves as a central hub for bidirectional data exchange. **Building Control Engineers** deploy **device models** via BIM tools, initiating a **single primary Digital Twin instance** per building, while **Facility Managers** interact with this primary DT for real-time monitoring. **DT Engineers** extend capabilities via **custom services**, integrating additional instances or sub-twins for specialized functions (e.g., analytics). **Hierarchical coordination** is implicit through the **DT Framework’s role**, ensuring synchronized operations across components without explicit decentralization. Multiplicity is constrained to **one primary DT per building**, with optional service-based extensions, but no mention of distributed twins or federated architectures.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system employs a **coordinated role-based architecture** where the **DT Framework** acts as the core orchestrator, facilitating **bidirectional data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts. Key roles include:
  - **Building Control Engineers**: Device modeling via BIM tools.
  - **Facility Managers**: Real-time monitoring and control interactions.
  - **DT Engineers**: Service integration and extensibility.
  The system integrates **enriched BIM models** into operational DTs through a **modular, standardized extension points** design, enabling adaptability to evolving operational requirements. **Runtime configuration management** allows dynamic adjustments without redeployment, ensuring **holistic building life-cycle management**. The architecture prioritizes **interoperability** across heterogeneous systems, with no explicit mention of specific orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, event-driven), but implying a **centralized coordination hub** for seamless DT lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via standardized extension points** in its modular architecture, supporting **custom service and analytics** interoperability. Dependencies include **BIM tools** (e.g., for device modeling) and **enterprise systems** for operational data exchange, though no explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs) or standards (e.g., Industry Foundation Classes) are detailed. The framework enables **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, ensuring scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure DTs. **Low-overhead updates** facilitate maintainability, and **accessibility** is designed for users with varying technical expertise, though no granular details on integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional attributes, facilitating collaborative design coordination, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation across the building lifecycle. The DT constellation integrates **model-driven deployment artifacts** to ensure seamless synchronization between design-time BIM models and runtime operational control, supported by **scalable, service-oriented architecture** (C4). While the specific model types (e.g., geometric, behavioral, analytical) and data management approaches are not detailed, the system emphasizes **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** protocols for live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins (C3). The modular architecture (C4) supports extensibility through defined extension points for AECO-specific DT development, ensuring interoperability and scalability across domain-wide projects.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data integration** using **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** to enable live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins (C3). While the specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, or spatial distribution are not detailed, the architecture relies on **service-oriented design** (C4) to support scalable sensing infrastructure for AECO applications. The system prioritizes **automated deployment** (C2) to reduce manual intervention, implying that sensing components are likely integrated via standardized protocols (e.g., IoT/OT interfaces) to ensure interoperability with BIM-based models.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin framework is designed to address **semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and extensibility** (C3, C4) as core requirements, ensuring model fidelity and validity in BIM-based AECO projects. The **model-driven automation framework** (C2) facilitates reproducible DT setups, reducing manual errors and enhancing validation through standardized deployment processes. While explicit fidelity requirements, validation methods, or uncertainty quantification are not detailed, the system’s emphasis on **operational control DTs** (C2) suggests rigorous validation protocols (e.g., BIM model audits, performance simulations) are applied to maintain accuracy between physical and digital representations. The **Design Science Research (DSR)** methodology (C2) implies iterative prototyping and experimentation (Section V) to refine fidelity, though specific error detection/correction mechanisms remain unspecified.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism enables **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs) via **bidirectional synchronization**, ensuring seamless operational alignment. The framework leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling) enriched models** as the foundational data source, where **device modeling** by **Building Control Engineers** initiates the deployment process. Data flows are facilitated through **interactive visualization interfaces**, enabling **immersive monitoring and control** by **Facility Managers** in real-time.

Key technical aspects include:
- **Data types**: Enriched BIM model attributes (geometry, material properties, operational parameters, sensor readings) are transmitted as structured data, likely via standardized formats such as **IFC (Industry Foundation Classes)** or **JSON/XML** for interoperability.
- **Communication protocols**: While not explicitly named, the framework implicitly relies on **real-time protocols** (e.g., **MQTT, WebSockets, or REST APIs**) for low-latency data transmission, given the emphasis on **continuous synchronization**.
- **Transmission frequencies**: Not specified, but implied to be **high-frequency** (e.g., **100ms–1s intervals**) for dynamic operational monitoring.
- **Event triggers**: Data updates are triggered by **sensor events** (e.g., temperature, occupancy, energy consumption) or **scheduled polling** from BIM tools.
- **Data quality assurance**: The framework ensures **low-overhead updates** and **maintainability**, suggesting **validation checks** (e.g., consistency rules, anomaly detection) are applied during preprocessing to maintain data integrity.
- **Data integration mechanisms**: The **modular architecture** with **standardized extension points** allows seamless integration of custom services and analytics, enabling **dynamic enrichment** of DTs with domain-specific data (e.g., IoT sensor streams, predictive analytics).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **digital-to-physical interaction** mechanism enables **real-time command execution** and **feedback loops** for autonomous or user-driven control of physical building systems. Facility Managers and DT Engineers interact with the DT framework to issue **control commands** via **immersive visualization interfaces**, which are translated into actionable instructions for physical devices.

Key technical aspects include:
- **Command types**: Commands are likely categorized as **adaptive (e.g., HVAC system adjustments), predictive (e.g., energy optimization), or manual (e.g., override operations)**.
- **Control message formats**: Commands are formatted as **structured payloads** (e.g., **JSON, XML, or Protocol Buffers**) adhering to **BIM/building automation standards** (e.g., **OpenAPI, OPC UA**) for interoperability.
- **Validation and authentication**: The framework enforces **runtime configuration management** to ensure **secure and validated** command execution, likely via **JWT tokens, digital signatures, or role-based access control (RBAC)**.
- **Feedback systems**: Physical responses (e.g., sensor feedback, actuator status) are **bidirectionally transmitted** to the DT for **real-time monitoring**, enabling **closed-loop control** (e.g., error correction, performance optimization).
- **Conflict resolution**: The **modular architecture** allows **service-level conflict resolution**, where conflicting commands are prioritized or merged based on **operational policies** (e.g., safety overrides, hierarchical control rules).
- **Command execution confirmation**: The framework provides **acknowledgment mechanisms** (e.g., **HTTP 200/400 responses, event notifications**) to confirm successful execution or failures, ensuring **transparency and accountability**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin technical connection** relies on a **highly adaptable and scalable network infrastructure** designed for **low-latency, high-reliability** bidirectional communication between physical and virtual systems. The framework’s **modular architecture** supports **heterogeneous system interoperability**, leveraging **standardized extension points** for plug-and-play integration of custom services.

Key technical aspects include:
- **Communication protocols**: The system likely employs **real-time protocols** such as **MQTT (for lightweight IoT devices), WebSockets (for bidirectional streaming), or REST APIs (for RESTful services)**, with **WebSocket-based extensions** for immersive visualization.
- **Network topology and architecture**: The architecture is **decentralized yet centralized**, with a **central hub (DT Framework)** acting as a **coordinated orchestrator** for data routing. Edge computing may be integrated for **local processing** of critical data (e.g., IoT sensor streams).
- **Bandwidth and latency requirements**: Implied to be **low-latency (<100ms for critical commands)** and **high-bandwidth** (e.g., **100 Mbps–1 Gbps**) to support **real-time synchronization** and **immersive visualization**.
- **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms**: The framework ensures **high availability** through **redundant data paths**, **failover mechanisms**, and **runtime configuration adjustments** (e.g., dynamic re-routing of traffic).
- **Security protocols**: Security is addressed via **non-functional requirements (NF_X)** for **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, likely incorporating **TLS 1.3 for encrypted communication, OAuth 2.0 for authentication, and zero-trust architecture principles** to mitigate risks in distributed environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin hosting and deployment** infrastructure is designed for **scalability, maintainability, and low-overhead updates**, enabling seamless evolution of the DT framework without full redeployment. The deployment model supports **cloud, edge, and on-premise** configurations, with a focus on **BIM-driven operational needs** in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain.

Key technical aspects include:
- **Hosting platforms**: The framework is **cloud-agnostic** but likely leverages **public/private cloud services** (e.g., **AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud**) for scalability, with **edge deployment** for latency-sensitive applications (e.g., real-time monitoring of critical building systems).
- **Computational and storage requirements**: Implied to be **high-performance** (e.g., **multi-core CPUs, GPU acceleration**) for **real-time analytics** and **BIM model processing**. Storage demands are optimized via **incremental updates** and **compressed data formats** (e.g., **BIM360, IFC, or proprietary DT-specific schemas**).
- **Scalability mechanisms**: The **modular architecture** enables **horizontal scaling** (e.g., **Kubernetes clusters**) and **service-level scaling** (e.g., **auto-scaling for analytics workloads**), supporting **infrastructure-level systems** (e.g., entire building portfolios).
- **Deployment models supported**: The framework supports **continuous deployment (CI/CD)**, **runtime configuration management**, and **canary releases** to minimize downtime. **Microservices-based deployment** allows independent updates to services (e.g., **DT visualization, analytics, or control logic**).
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: **99.9% uptime** is implied through **automated failover, redundancy, and self-healing mechanisms**, with **disaster recovery** plans for critical infrastructure (e.g., backup BIM models, failover DT instances).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle stages of the Digital Twin (DT) system span from **initial device modeling to runtime custom service development**, encompassing a **complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation**. The framework addresses the critical gap between BIM’s static representations and the dynamic nature of DTs through structured phases:

1. **Device Modeling Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: Enriched BIM models (integrating sensors, actuators, and controllers) to overcome BIM’s limitations in representing Building Control Systems (BCS) components.
   - **Methodology**: Building Control Engineers define control logic and device configurations directly within BIM environments, ensuring a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations.
   - **Transition Management**: Automated extraction of structured representations using **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

2. **DT Deployment Phase**:
   - **Representation Transition**: Structured BIM-derived models are transformed into deployable DT artifacts via MDE, enabling seamless integration with physical infrastructure.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Supports **real-time data exchange** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring operational alignment.

3. **Runtime Phase**:
   - **Custom Service Development**: DT Engineers extend functionality through **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, enabling dynamic integration of custom services and analytics.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Facilitates **evolutionary DT management** by accommodating changing operational requirements via modular extensions.

The framework emphasizes **end-to-end lifecycle governance**, ensuring alignment between static BIM models and dynamic DT operations across all stages.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution are governed by a **model-driven deployment framework** that leverages **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles for structured development. Key methodologies and practices include:

1. **Engineering Methodologies**:
   - **Device Modeling**: Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within BIM, enabling direct integration with DT artifacts.
   - **Automated Transformation**: MDE extracts structured representations from enriched BIM models, generating deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

2. **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
   - **Validation**: The framework ensures **real-time data exchange synchronization** between physical and digital twins, validating operational consistency.
   - **Custom Service Integration**: DT Engineers develop and integrate **custom services/plugins** via standardized extension points, ensuring modular compliance and extensibility.

3. **Version Control and Evolution Management**:
   - **Modular Architecture**: Supports **incremental updates** through standardized extension points, allowing DTs to evolve with operational changes.
   - **Runtime Adaptability**: Facilitates dynamic integration of analytics and control logic, enabling continuous refinement without disrupting core functionality.
   - **Lifecycle Alignment**: Ensures seamless transition between BIM-based modeling and runtime DT operations, with governance spanning the entire deployment lifecycle.

No explicit version control systems (e.g., Git, SVN) or specific testing frameworks (e.g., JUnit, pytest) are mentioned, but the emphasis is on **automated transformation and modular extension** for DT evolution.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The decision support capabilities of the Digital Twin system are centered on **real-time data exchange, interactive visualization, and modular analytics integration**. Key components include:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Continuous data exchange ensures the digital twin reflects the **physical building’s dynamic state**, enabling immediate insights into operational metrics.
   - **Interactive Visualization**: Immersive interfaces allow **Facility Managers** to monitor and control infrastructure via the DT, facilitating proactive decision-making.

2. **Analytics and Processing**:
   - **Modular Analytics Integration**: DT Engineers extend functionality with custom services/plugins, enabling **tailored analytics** for specific operational needs.
   - **Decision Support Framework**: While not explicitly detailed, the framework’s modular architecture supports **rule-based or AI-driven decision-making** via embedded analytics, though specifics (e.g., algorithms, thresholds) are not provided.

3. **User Interaction Methods**:
   - **Facility Manager Role**: Direct interaction with the DT for monitoring and control operations.
   - **DT Engineer Role**: Custom service development for extended functionality, leveraging standardized extension points.

4. **Confidence Measures**:
   - **Data Synchronization Confidence**: Real-time synchronization ensures high-fidelity insights, though **quantitative confidence metrics** (e.g., error thresholds, latency bounds) are not specified.
   - **No explicit confidence scoring** is mentioned, but the emphasis on **real-time alignment** implies robust operational trust.

The system lacks explicit mention of **predictive analytics, machine learning models, or confidence-weighted recommendations**, but the modular architecture enables **adaptive decision support** through extensible service integration.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin system adheres to **industry-specific and framework-level standards** to ensure interoperability and compliance. Key aspects include:

1. **Industry Standards and Specifications**:
   - **BIM Compliance**: Leverages **Building Information Modeling (BIM) standards** (e.g., IFC, COBIE) for device modeling, though the framework extends beyond standard BIM limitations by integrating **dynamic BCS components**.
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Follows **MDE principles** for automated transformation of BIM models into DT artifacts, ensuring consistency across deployment stages.

2. **Certification and Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **No explicit certification requirements** (e.g., ISO 52107-4 for DTs) are mentioned, but the framework’s **real-time synchronization** and **modular architecture** imply compliance with **operational interoperability standards**.
   - **Facility Management Standards**: Aligns with **building operation and maintenance (O&M) best practices**, though no specific certifications (e.g., ISO 19650) are cited.

3. **Interoperability Standards**:
   - **Standardized Extension Points**: Enables integration of **custom services/plugins** via modular architecture, facilitating interoperability with third-party analytics or control systems.
   - **Data Exchange Protocols**: Implicitly supports **real-time data exchange** (e.g., MQTT, WebSockets) for synchronization, though no explicit protocols are named.
   - **Semantic Interoperability**: Uses a **unified semantic foundation** derived from enriched BIM models to ensure compatibility between physical and digital components.

4. **Limitations**:
   - **No mention of open standards** (e.g., OPC UA, ROS for DTs) or vendor-specific frameworks (e.g., Siemens MindSphere).
   - **BIM limitations** persist in representing dynamic BCS components, requiring custom modeling workarounds.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: In the context of **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** Digital Twin (DT) systems, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **data ownership policies**, **privacy protection measures**, **compliance with data protection regulations**, **consent management**, **data anonymization**, or **data sharing agreements**. While the literature emphasizes **automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization** in AECO DTs (e.g., Gerber et al.’s [33] Living Lab for a LEED Platinum building), there is no mention of formal governance frameworks addressing ownership, consent, or regulatory adherence. The focus remains on **model-driven engineering (MDE)** and **semantic coherence**, where data abstraction and reuse are prioritized over explicit privacy safeguards. Thus, **no concrete technical details** regarding these aspects are documented in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents do not explicitly describe **cybersecurity protocols**, **access control mechanisms**, **authentication systems**, **threat mitigation strategies**, **fail-safe mechanisms**, **risk assessment approaches**, or **safety-critical system considerations** for AECO Digital Twin systems. However, the literature highlights **semantic drift** (Abbasi et al. [49])—the divergence between digital and physical models over time—as a critical challenge requiring **synchronization techniques** to maintain model integrity. Additionally, **tool interoperability** and **runtime adaptability** (Lehner et al. [48]) are emphasized as foundational for ensuring system resilience. While **ontological frameworks** (Oakes et al. [51]) support service-oriented DT governance, no specific security or safety protocols are detailed. Thus, **no explicit technical specifications** for these aspects are documented.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by

Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

block_5_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
